name: domain-crispr-synthesis
description: Use web_search to search for 'CRISPR gene editing future outlook 2026 2030 applications promise'. Results overlap with earlier CRISPR searches. Synthesize the CRISPR outlook: most promising applications, remaining barriers, and timeline to broader clinical use. Keep response under 100 words.

ground_truth.reference_answer: Promising applications: CRISPR-based therapies are most advanced for blood disorders (sickle cell, beta-thalassemia), with expanding trials in cardiovascular disease, hereditary angioedema, cancer immunotherapy via engineered CAR-T cells, and the first personalized in vivo treatments for ultra-rare diseases. Key barriers: Off-target editing risks, limited in vivo delivery beyond the liver, Cas9 immunogenicity, manufacturing complexity driving $2.2M per-patient costs, reduced venture capital investment causing company layoffs and pipeline contractions, and unresolved ethical concerns around germline editing. Timeline: By 2027-2028, expect approvals for hereditary angioedema and ATTR amyloidosis; by 2030, potentially a dozen approved CRISPR therapies spanning blood disorders, liver diseases, and select cancers, with base and prime editing entering late-stage trials.
key_facts: Blood disorders lead CRISPR therapy development, with cardiovascular, cancer, and rare disease applications expanding
Major barriers include off-target effects, liver-centric delivery, immunogenicity, and $2.2M per-patient costs
Approvals for hereditary angioedema and ATTR amyloidosis are expected by 2027-2028
The number of approved gene editing treatments could grow from one to potentially a dozen by 2030
Reduced venture capital and high trial costs have led to layoffs and pipeline contractions across CRISPR companies
